question_text: Will Brazil win the match?
postmortem_note: RECOMMENDED 0.58-0.60 (RULE1: avg of Smarkets 57.87% market and predicted crowd-inflation). SUBMITTED 1.00 instead - a personal-conviction override, not the researched number. Brazil drew 1-1 (did not win). RBP=-51.97 is our worst-ever single result, exceeding QAT-SUI Q1's -42.51. Crowd (0.67) was ALSO too high (as our crowd-inflation prediction anticipated) but still beat us by a huge margin because 100% guarantees squared error of exactly 1.0 when wrong, with no possible upside beyond what ~0.90-0.95 would have given if right. Counterfactual: at us=0.60, sq_err=0.36 vs crowd's sq_err=0.4489 - we would have BEATEN crowd. Even RULE1's literal avg(0.67,0.5787)=0.624 would have beaten crowd (sq_err=0.389). ANY of the researched numbers wins this question; only the 100% override loses it.

question_text: Will the match be tied at halftime?
postmortem_note: Recommended 0.45 (market, Draw-at-HT normalized to 44.57%), submitted 0.32 (-13pp, moving AWAY from both market and crowd). Outcome was YES (HT was tied) - this deviation went the WRONG direction, likely the same 'Brazil will dominate, so no tie' gut feeling that produced Q8's override. Had we submitted the recommended 0.45, sq_err=(1-0.45)^2=0.3025 would have beaten crowd's sq_err=(1-0.42)^2=0.3364, turning this into a small win.

question_text: Will Sweden commit more fouls than Tunisia?
postmortem_note: OUR 3RD-WORST RESULT EVER. Outcome=YES: Sweden 10 fouls > Tunisia 8. Sweden's 10 is close to their own n=5 average (8.6) - Sweden played roughly true to form. Tunisia's 8 is WAY below their n=5 average (15.4, range 9-29) - Tunisia's historical foul rate did NOT transfer to this match. Sweden won 5-1 (a blowout); Tunisia, on the wrong end of it, fouled far less than usual (disengagement/'blowout fatigue', same mechanism as GER-CUR's Curacao going 19min without a foul after falling further behind). See RULE10 in project_crowd_bias_model.md - this is now 2/2 (GER-CUR, SWE-TUN): in BLOWOUTS, the WINNING team fouls >= the LOSING team, regardless of which team's historical average was higher.

AUSTRIA HAD MORE CORNERS THAN ARGENTINA IN A 2-0 LOSS. Corner reversal: Austria chased from 1-0 deficit throughout match, repeatedly attacked, got repelled by ARG, accumulated corners. ARG at 2-0 sat back and defended — didn't need corners. LESSON: in competitive match where trailing team chases late, CHASING team accumulates corners from failed attacks. 'Dominant team gets more corners' only holds in genuine blowouts where dominant team keeps attacking. In a managed 2-0, the chasing team is the one attacking into corners.

ARG-AUT was 2-0, Austria fighting hard to come back from 1-0 deficit. Competitive underdog context → more physical play → more cards. SAME ROOT CAUSE AS Q9 SABITZER: we modeled this as a blowout when it was a competitive match. Both Q6 AND Q9 failed for the same reason.

ARG scored 2 goals but had FEWER THAN 6 SOT. This is now a pattern: AUT scored 3 in AUT-JOR with <6 SOT, ARG scored 2 here with <6 SOT. Elite teams in managed wins are CLINICAL — high goal-to-SOT conversion, low total SOT. SOT multiplier of 3.3-3.7x lambda may be too high. Revise down to 2.8-3.0x for elite teams expected to win by 2-goal margin.

Jordan more fouls than Algeria?
postmortem_note: CATASTROPHIC MISS. Our strongest call (0.32 TIER 1 STRONG NO) was completely wrong. Jordan committed MORE fouls than Algeria despite Algeria averaging 14.78/game vs Jordan's 10.44/game. ROOT CAUSE: We used static base-rate foul data without accounting for the actual match script. Jordan scored first → led 1-0 at HT → spent 2H DEFENDING against desperate Algeria attacks → Jordan fouls more DEFENDING their lead. Algeria, the pressing team in base rates, was now ATTACKING with the ball in 2H — attacking teams don't commit fouls, they draw them. The match script flipped the foul dynamic completely. THIS IS A STRUCTURAL RULE FAILURE, not just variance.

question_text: 2H had 2+ goals?
postmortem_note: In a 5-0 win, 2H almost certainly had 2+ goals (either Portugal piled on or match totals required it). We said 0.42, crowd said 0.52 — crowd was right. ROOT CAUSE: When total expected goals is 5+, the 2H goal distribution analysis changes. We should have anchored on TOTAL EXPECTED GOALS (market had over 3.5 at 46%) rather than the λ_2H model alone. If total is likely 4-5 goals, P(2H has 2+) is much higher than 42%.

Biggest loss of the match. We anchored hard to Ecuador's '0 goals in 2 WC2026 matches' finishing crisis and a market-bounded ceiling (OU2.5=33.9%), but Ecuador finally converted in their must-win finale, scoring twice. The 0-goal streak was a small-sample artifact (16 SOT, 0 goals = unsustainable conversion luck), not a durable skill signal — we over-weighted it.

Sweden's offside consistency (3 offsides in BOTH GD1 win and GD2 loss) held a third time. The single highest-conviction pick of the day paid off biggest — direct confirmation of the structural λ=3.0 pattern.

THE KEY MISS. Cape Verde sat on exactly 1 offside for almost the entire match — consistent with our cagey-match read — then picked up a 2nd in stoppage time pushing for a late winner in a still-0-0, must-have-points game. Root cause: we shaded DOWN from the raw Poisson (0.44) to 0.32 based on a 'fewer transition forays in an even matchup' narrative. The raw number was closer to truth than our shaded one, AND we missed a distinct pattern — late-match desperation in a scoreless draw between two teams that both need a result raises stoppage-time offside risk, similar in spirit to chasing-a-deficit (BRA-HAI, POR-UZB, TUR-PAR) but triggered by a tied scoreline + mutual must-win pressure rather than an actual deficit. See updated STEPS_FOR_HIGH_POINTS.md entry.

The lesson 'this exact player was suppressed before' does NOT transfer when the team's entire attacking ceiling is shattered by match context; RULE15 precedent on an individual player is only as good as the team-context assumptions underneath it.

We floored this at 0.30 per FLOOR_0.25 logic, treating Germany's 0-offside record as suppressive — but per Q15, regulation itself was tense and low-scoring (<3 goals), meaning this was a high-pressing, transition-heavy, low-conversion 90 minutes (lots of attacking forays breaking down at the back line) rather than a clinical match. The crowd's 0.53 correctly priced in that kind of grinding, end-to-end-without-finishing match shape that our floor-based model, anchored to Germany's clean group-stage offside record, didn't weight heavily enough. CRITICAL: FLOOR_0.25 prevents going TOO low but doesn't protect against a genuinely scrappy, high-pressing match blowing past a moderate floor.
